item.title: The Case for the Great American Road Trip This Summer
item.body:
## Why the drive is having a moment

With summer fully underway, the classic road trip is looking better than ever. Flights can be crowded and pricey during peak weeks, but a car gives you something planes can't: flexibility, spontaneity, and the freedom to stop wherever the view demands it. You don't need a distant destination to make it worthwhile — some of the best trips are three tanks of gas from home.

## Planning that actually pays off

A little structure makes the open road far more enjoyable.

- **Anchor the trip around one big stop.** A national park, a coastline, a city you've never explored. Build the rest of the route around it rather than trying to see everything.
- **Cap your daily driving.** Six hours behind the wheel is a good ceiling. Beyond that, the scenery blurs and the fun fades. Leave room to wander.
- **Book the first and last nights.** Reserve lodging for the bookend nights and stay loose in between. That balance keeps the trip both safe and spontaneous.
- **Download maps offline.** Cell coverage vanishes in the most beautiful places. Save your route and key stops before you lose signal.

## Beating the summer heat and crowds

Late-June travel means sun, and lots of it. A few habits go a long way:

1. **Start early.** Popular trailheads and viewpoints are calm and cool at 8 a.m. and packed by noon.
2. **Hydrate like it's your job.** Keep a full water bottle per person within reach, and refill at every stop.
3. **Respect the midday lull.** Use the hottest hours for driving, lunch, or a shaded museum rather than strenuous hikes.

## The underrated joy of the in-between

The secret of a good road trip is that the destination is only half the point. The roadside diner with the handwritten pie menu, the overlook you found by accident, the town whose name you'll forget but whose ice cream you won't — those unplanned moments are what people remember years later.

So fill the tank, build a playlist, and give yourself permission to take the slower road. Summer is short, the country is wide, and the best stories rarely happen at the places you circled on the map.
